speaker: interviewer
text: With Mila Bachner. It's July 25, 1995. Mila, you were telling us that you were in your sister's room. They were selecting the mothers and babies.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember they did not allow me to go with her when they rounded up all these young women. And I know many of them, because they were my cousins who had a baby-- who had babies, and friends, you know, neighbors' children who had, who had babies. And my sister said to me, Mila, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], because they will take it with me. I said, maybe it's better that I go with you. She says, Mila, mommy is still home. You cannot do that. And they took them down to the great synagogue in [INAUDIBLE]. I will show you the picture later of the great synagogue if I have a chance to get up and and take down them. I did not stay home. And I didn't go home. I went down to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means the synagogue street. Because the shul was there, they called it the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And we had a friend who had an office across from the shul. He was a German Jew. He lived in Berlin but came to Chrzanow. And he was very, a very brilliant guy, you know. So they allowed him to have an office. He worked for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him-- he died in Florida four years ago. So I said to him, Mr. Prista, I must hide here. Because my sister is there. My cousins are there, the people that were just brought. He said, he said, of course you can. And I-- and he allowed me to be in the office. They took them at night, of course, you know, so I slept in the office for, you know? And then I looked out, like it could've been maybe 1, 2 o'clock in the morning. And you know, lights are burning there by the shuls. So I see my sister throwing down her pocketbook. In that pocketbook, she had the pictures of the baby. She put in her, her engagement ring. So I said, Herr Prista, Mr. Prista, I have to run out. I want to make a run for the pocketbook. So as I'm leaving the office and making-- trying to run, my sister says to me, Mila, go back. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She says, Mila [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She knew that if they see, if they see me that they will either kill me or take me with her, which I would have loved to go with her. And I went back. But you know something? A [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and one of my-- Marishka, a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] from my school, she, she made a run for it. I didn't know what the heck she was doing at this hour on this street. But she ran, and she picked up the pocketbook.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, Marishka, just give me the pictures. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I said only the pictures of the baby-- I don't want anything else. She ran away so quickly. Like a lightning, she disappeared. And that was it. It was about 4 o'clock in the morning maybe. And I see the women are starting to march down with their babies in arms from the shul. It-- they were in the upstairs. It was like the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], where the men were downstairs and the women upstairs. And I used to daven there with Mommy. I used to sit, you know, there. And I see these beautiful mothers with their babies in their arms, how they are being. Also, the, the sidewalk by the shul was already lined up with trucks. And on each truck on the panel was written Auschwitz, so I knew [? about ?] the destination, as they all already knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were pushed into those trucks with such force, kicked in, those who couldn't. There was a, a step to go on the truck, you know? And those who could make it with the baby-- one, one had more than one child. There were three and four children with some mothers. They were kicked in. The Gestapo with the boots gave them a kick in the, in the [INAUDIBLE]. And they flew in like [INAUDIBLE] the babies out of their hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long it took. But then the truck, the truck started to, to move. And, and this was last that I ever saw of my sister. So she was there with her husband and the baby. They found him, too. And they went together. This was the end of my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came home. And I said to my sister, [? Mesia, ?] I told her what happened. She cried [? never ?] bitterly, you know? Because [CRYING] every, every time, every other day, there was somebody else taken from us. And you see such a beautiful family being so dismembered one by one. And you ask me how I go on living? I'm asking myself the same question. I live. I try to joke. I try to, I try to be human. But you know, my heart [INAUDIBLE]. My heart is so bitter. It's, it's a hole that will never be mended-- never, what they did to us. [CRYING] I told my sister, [? Mesia-- ?] I said, you know, I have to go to work to [INAUDIBLE]. Because if I don't go-- I missed already a few, a few shifts, you know-- I said they'll find out I'm missing for so many days. They'll come. And they'll, they'll take and kill us all. And then I said, you know, maybe this will be a good idea. They would-- maybe daddy, and I, and you, and my brother-- we'll go together, and then, you know? So then, so-- and then I said, you know, that's not such a good idea, [INAUDIBLE]. I'm going to go to work. And it was on a Friday night. And I had to do a night shift. And when did I ever cook on a, on a Friday night? When did I ever drive on a car-- on a car-- in a, on a train on a Friday night? But times have changed. Our Lord, blessed be His name, closed his eyes. He did not want to see anything that is happening to us. And I don't know why. He could have punished the bad people. Why punish beautiful, innocent babies and, and, and, and rabbis? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said to me, Mila, I have a very bad feeling if you go to work today. I don't know why. Don't ask me how I can feel [INAUDIBLE]. She said, I have a feeling I will not see you again. I said, sweetie, I said, I am, I am working for the Germans. I have my [INAUDIBLE]. I said, they wouldn't do anything to me. She said, Mila, I have a very bad feeling that I will not see you again. I took a little-- a little-- it was a night shift, so I took a little food, I, whatever I had, a potato I made. I don't know what I made for them to have something to eat. And I went to work. I took the train, and I went to [INAUDIBLE]. I'm holding my little food under my arm. And I come to [INAUDIBLE]. And I'm-- you know, this is, this is how the, the-- I am walking in that little alley, like you know, to the factory. Here is like a fence. And I am passing by this fence. And something grabs me by my arm. You know, like something grabs me by my arm. And I could not see who it was. I didn't-- I had no idea. And then I see that this man goes-- you know, like, he shows me for the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took this food. And I gave it to him. And he ran away so quickly. I found out that these were already French prisoners, Jewish-French prisoners, brought from-- to, to our town from either Auschwitz or whatever. I don't know whatever happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought to myself, I will go home. And I will, I will eat whatever I have. This poor guy is starving. He, you know-- and this was it. I came to the factory. I start working.

speaker: interviewer
text: At about 12 o'clock, midnight, there is a voice over the loud speaker, which tells us to behave in perfect order. We don't want [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We do not want any trouble makers. Those who will cause any kind of troubles will be taken behind the factory outside. And they will be shot with a gun inaudible to others. They use those silencers, you know? And our IDs are being, you know, taken away from us. And I started to cry. I said, God almighty, my beautiful little sister. She had such a feeling for me not to go. Take a look sometimes a premonition of a person. If I would have listened to her, I would have gone maybe to Auschwitz with them, would've been better. And then I wanted to live, because if they were-- if we all die, who is going to tell what happened to us? Who is going to talk? [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't come home. And we marched from [INAUDIBLE]. Well, what happened is that people did become very unruly. They became scared, petrified. And, of course, they cried. And some of them screamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before you know it, it was like peace, quiet. They did take out those people who caused, who caused unrest. And as I'm walking out from the factory, I see like beautiful red blood is flowing in the-- like, you know, from behind to building-- beautiful, red blood. And I said, what could-- you know, blood? So I go. I, I go out of the-- of my line. And I go behind the building. And there they lay there, these poor beautiful children, shot to death, exactly what they did. What they promised, they did. They killed them. We walked all the way to [INAUDIBLE], to the, to the train station. And my sister must have heard that we are going to be passing through, you know? So my sister and my little brother-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I didn't tell you when they took Mommy. They took Mommy before me. I have to go back again a little bit. Please forgive me, little-- Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: After my-- after they took my sister, I came home. And we hear that there is going to be a roundup on, on mothers, OK? And I think that this was already the last straw. If they take Mommy from us, then surely our life is not anymore important at all. This will be it. And the Gestapo comes. It was also in the nighttime, of course. They-- I don't know if they were-- if we were allowed to put anything on us or not, or it was in the nightgowns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't remember the month, either. I don't. But I know it was in 1940-- beginning of 1942. This much, I do remember, because I was taken in August of 1942, so it was very close, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister and I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to see this picture you have there on the table, where I am there with my sister and Mommy. My sister is holding Mommy's hand once on the right side. I'm holding Mommy's hand on the left side. And we are going. And this Gestapo said [GERMAN SPEECH]-- no children. And I said, [GERMAN SPEECH]. I gave him the title [GERMAN SPEECH] for like over, like higher than the SS. I didn't know what I said, but I used the word. I read it. And he says [GERMAN SPEECH]. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And I said, I will not-- [GERMAN SPEECH]. I want to go with my mommy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? This is how we were positioned, only not in this close. That's a wedding picture for my sister. My mommy wore maybe a nightgown. I held onto Mommy. I was shivering. And my sister held onto Mommy on this side. And this is how. Finally, he saw that he will not get away with it. He said to me, OK, you want to go? Go. You know, he'll, he'll do what he has to do there, you know? And with all the other women-- my mommy was 47 years old here. And as you see, she wore this wig that made her look much older. They didn't make such elegant wigs in those days, you know? She was very religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started our march down to the marketplace. When we came there, we are standing. And we are shivering. I knew-- I felt that my heart was going to jump out of my chest cavity. This is how I, this is how I felt. And, and I was crying. And over to us comes this Gestapo. He must have been six feet tall-- that, that, that coat, and those black boots, and that, that helmet. It, it gave me the, the shivers just to look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says to me, [GERMAN SPEECH]? Why are crying? I said, I'm not crying. I have a cold. So he says to me, why are you shivering? [GERMAN SPEECH]. So I said, because I'm cold. He knew, he knew well what is happening with me, you know? And I-- and Mommy is standing there. And I see, and I see that our moments are counted before they take this lady from us. And you know what, Elke? That in all the sufferings that I saw and endured but what they did to this lady, to my mommy-- [CRYING] I say I don't hate. I don't want to hate. But I only pray to God that in His, in His infinite wisdom, He should pay them back a little bit of what they did to human beings.

speaker: interviewee
text: They deserve to be repaid. And you know, they will get paid back. I do believe that justice grants slowly, but it grants evenly. And they will get it back. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: My mommy wore this wig. Then he takes off the rifle off his shoulder, you know? And with the butt of the rifle, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he tries to pull off her wig. And in the process of doing so, he made a deep gash in her forehead going through, you know? And the wig remained on his butt. Her whole face got covered with blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to scream in pain. And we said, Mommy, you know that he-- I, I screamed help, help. [GERMAN SPEECH]. Let somebody come help Mommy. But there was nobody there to do so. Because our lives were not-- our lives were cheap. Our-- we were not human. We were not human beings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he cut me off with his gloved hand on one side, threw me to this side. My little sister was thrown that way. And mommy was thrown on top of the other women. [INAUDIBLE], you know? Just thrown like, like-- she was, she couldn't see anything. She was probably-- she probably bled to death on the train where they took her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- my beautiful mommy-- and he said to us to go home. So we didn't go home. My sister and I became-- we were both very hysterical. We, we didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: We talked about killing each other. So then I had the little brother home with my father. You know, how can you just go ahead and take your life, you know? It, it-- we followed those ladies down to the train station, the train, the same train station that way [? that runs. ?] But theirs was a cattle wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all this-- and I saw two women were holding mommy up, because she could not see anymore what she's doing. She was all covered with blood. So they helped her up on the steps to the train. And you know, they could not close the doors. Because the people-- you know, it was too, too much. So they use the rope, they shouldn't fall out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't know if Mommy saw us, but I didn't see her either. She must have been laying there [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And then when-- then we went home. When we came home, we told Daddy-- Daddy asked us, where's Mommy? And we had to tell him.

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as we told him what happened to Mommy, he fell to the floor. And he, he became paralyzed. A total paralysis came over him. It, it was like he was stricken with some kind of a muscular dystrophy or something. I don't know. He couldn't move. He couldn't-- he was totally, like, you know, like doubled up. I couldn't call a doctor. Because if I call a doctor, they would not come and help. They would-- to a hospital, they wouldn't take him. And then they would send the German. And maybe the Polack alone himself will kill him. So we kept him home for as long as we were together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, I went to work. This is where I'm going back, when I went to work the [? Trebinje ?], and should I repeat all that? And I was-- and our, our IDs were taken from us. And, and my sister told me not to go. And I did go. And they killed those poor kids behind the building. And then we walked from [? Trebinje ?] [INAUDIBLE] to the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister must have found out with my brother that we are going to pass through. My father could not come, because he was totally paralyzed. So only my aunt came. I saw an aunt yet from-- that was still in Chrzanow and, and a, a cousin, an older, an older cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they brought me some food, whatever. And my sister, my little brother, Nahman, came over to me. And they kissed me and hugged me. And she says to me, Mila, I told you. I said, [GERMAN SPEECH], how was I to know, sweetheart? How was I to know? And she was so [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She was in such pain yet about Mommy. She was beyond control. And I said to her-- and my little brother says to me, Mila-- hugging me and kissing me-- he says to me, Mila, the mirror from the house, they are taking from us now. I was the mirror in the house, like you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, [INAUDIBLE], [GERMAN SPEECH]. Daddy, and [INAUDIBLE], and you take care of each other. I said, the world will have to end sometimes. And then we will get together. So we lived like in denial. Because I did not want to believe that this will not happen. But deep in my heart, I knew that it, it's impossible, that this, that this can-- that they will heal Mommy. But of all the pain that I endured-- and I mean this with all my heart-- the most agonizing moment was when they took Mommy from me. This I thought was already the end of me, too. [CRYING] I don't, I don't know how long I was on the, in the train. When they put us on the trains, it was not a cattle wagon. It was a passenger train. We were there maybe for two days until we came to Neusalz, maybe a day a and a half. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: I sat on the train with that little food that my aunt had given me. And I'm holding my head in my hand. And I'm saying to myself, where are you going, Mila? Who is left for you, with you? You have no brother. You have no sister. You have two little kids at home, a sick father. Why? Why did people do this to other people? Why do they inflict so much pain? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long I sat. I cried. And I sat. And I cried. And I talked to myself. I had no answers. And then we came to-- we finally-- the, the train finally came to a stop. And I see this big factory. I opened up my like out, you know, of a bad dream, something. And I give a look. And I see this is big factory, you know, with those iron gates.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I look out. And I said, what could this be? And then I see the sign over the, over the gate. And it said in German, [GERMAN SPEECH]. It is not important that you live as long as you do your duty. This is the, the translation of, of-- like in Auschwitz, they had [GERMAN SPEECH]. Here, they had this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We got out of the, of the train. Again, the Nazis informed us that we must be at our best behavior. There was already the [GERMAN SPEECH]. You know, the Nazi, Nazi women we had. They were worse than the men, I want you to know, in case you didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neusalz [INAUDIBLE]. We were constantly under Nazi guard. And these gates opened up. And I saw people emaciated-- peoples' living skeletons. I never in my life believed that this was-- that this is human. They looked like skeletons who, you know, you see them on those like the-- like the-- what do you call this? Puppets, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And they started to attack us. They saw we are carrying something. So what I did-- I took this food. And I just threw it at them. I figured, what do I need this? I want to live. I came already here. I don't want to die now by, by, by them, you know? So they went away with the food. And we walked into the factory. It was a slave labor camp. So I worked in a factory by [INAUDIBLE], you know, where we did cotton threads? I told you this, I think-- the whole thing. Shall I repeat the whole thing?

words.text: Shall I repeat the whole thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a factory where we used to work out cotton from scratch. So my hands were all day long in mud. You know, I had to put in-- my hand had to go in like this and catch the spool in case it ripped-- a thread ripped. With this, I had to catch to the spool, the spool. If I let it like this, then I lose my fingers. Or my hand would be cut off in the machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very careful. Nothing ever like this happened to me. And I would stop the machine. And I would attach that little cotton. And the-- and I would start the machine again. We didn't have enough food to eat. We did not have hot water to, to bathe. And we did not have enough water to drink. We had to be clean or else they would cut off your hair. And I had beautiful black hair-- pigtails like, like you ever wanted to see. I was a very beautiful child, you know? And, and I said to, to myself, if this is going to be my home, then I have to make the best of it. There's no use now to, to just-- I will have to carry on like all the other girls. And that's it. My thoughts were always with my brother, my sister, my, my, my [INAUDIBLE] sister, [INAUDIBLE]. What did they do with her in Auschwitz? [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, the last transport came to, to Neusalz. And a friend of mine came. This was already judenrein, when they cleared out the town of Jews completely. So she, she gave me this picture of me, where I made the bigger one. She brought it to me to-- she took it from my sister and gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, Mila, I wanted [INAUDIBLE] to come with me. But she said she's not leaving Daddy and, and your little brother. And she went with them to Auschwitz. She said, Mila, [GERMAN SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: She was so beautiful. She was-- she would have been more beautiful than [INAUDIBLE]. That's what a beautiful child is grow up to be. So I didn't see her already a few-- at that time a few weeks, you know-- a few years, 19-- 1942, a year, really. And then in 1940-- then they all went to Auschwitz-- my dad, my sister, my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard that from someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Elsa told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elsa told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsa Blumenfeld-- she was this friend of mine who came with judenrein. But she still came to camp. They still picked out some girls for slave labor camp. And my life in, in the camp was I did very hard work. I told you. I went down to the toilet things. And I, I did everything for little food. And I could-- and I gave my friend half of what I got. I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and you were telling me, how did you clean the toilets?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went down. We let ourselves down with a rope to the, to the, to the drek, if you excuse me. And we cleaned this out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to go in and take a shower. Imagine, you had no hot water. So I scrubbed my, my skin off from me to keep myself clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was extra work?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was extra work. For this, I got a little extra soap. And then I gave [INAUDIBLE] a little bit of the soap, because she was emaciated. And she was from my hometown. And I wanted her to live. I didn't want her to die. She's alive. She's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and it was very, very hard work. I, you know, I needed a knife to cut the threads. When the machine was finished-- the spools-- we start the machine. And we cut it off. So my knife was already-- what do you call it? Stale? Dull.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dull. So I tried to cut it. And it didn't work so well. So I went over to the meister. And I said to him, Herr Meister, [GERMAN SPEECH]. My knife is not sharp enough. So he says, [GERMAN SPEECH]. Give me the knife.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he takes my knife. And he cuts into my finger, into this finger. And he says, [GERMAN SPEECH]. Your life is sharp. You have blood here.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it healed up so beautifully. He, he made me, a, a cut. But that's a very good spot to heal. It seems that it healed. [GERMAN SPEECH]. But I complained to, to, to the, to the [GERMAN SPEECH], to our camp eldest, who was a Nazi woman. She notified this to the, to the meister, Herr Meister Heinrich. And the other guy was fired and Herr Meister Heinrich came in. Heinrich was an anti-Semite, a Nazi like you ever wanted to see one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know-- worse. He was so mean. But this was his physique.

speaker: interviewer
text: Careful. Mila, be careful.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that's how he walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Be careful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot I have it on. OK That's how he walked. But you know, he took a liking to me. Elke, he called me the [GERMAN SPEECH], you black gypsy. I had a skin like olive, you know? I had a skin like you, [LAUGHS] a little bit lighter maybe, but-- and he, he adored me. He adored me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He watched me like a hawk. And he didn't give me anything. But he didn't beat me. That was good, you know? Did the other one beat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He cut my finger. That's, that's, that's good enough, I mean, enough [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back to camp. And then it was March 15 about when we hear. And over the loud speaker comes a voice that says [GERMAN SPEECH]. The camp of Neusalz is going to be evacuated because the [GERMAN SPEECH]. Because we need to make a hospital out of this camp for our German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: The truth is that they needed the camp as a front against the Russians, which meant that the Russians were closing in. And there was this big River Oder, you know? There was no escape from there. There was a lot of suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: People got killed. People got sent away to Auschwitz. For any little thing, they would, they would do that. But you know, I tried to keep my composure. I did everything that I should do and do it right, because I did not want any problems at this point. I just wanted to remain alive.
